fuzzy_prd:
We need a small collection of reusable utility helpers that our desktop application can use across many different screens and components. These helpers should cover a few everyday needs: cleaning up version label strings so they all look consistent, producing grammatically correct text when showing counts of things, turning a flat list of release records into a fast lookup table, ordering those releases so the newest ones always appear first (while still handling oddly-formatted labels gracefully), navigating and updating deeply nested settings objects using a simple path notation, building a visual split-panel arrangement from a list of panels and also reading that arrangement back into an ordered list, computing where a floating tip or detail card should appear on screen without going off-screen edges, and reordering action buttons in dialogs to match what the operating system expects. The version-cleaning logic should work the same way it already does in our existing string normalization module, and the button ordering behavior should mirror the platform-aware sorting we use elsewhere in our dialog management code. All helpers must be pure and dependency-light so they can be tested independently and composed freely. Outputs should be plain text or compact serialized data with no extra whitespace, and each result should end with a newline.

A couple small clarifications from the team chat so nobody has to guess. For the version cleanup piece, normalizeVersion behaves exactly like the one in golden/src/utils/normalize-version.ts: it strips only the lowercase letter 'v' when it appears as the very first character of the version string. If the first character is anything else, including uppercase 'V', it stays as-is. If the version argument is missing or undefined, it should default to ''. The function just returns that modified string, and output should be followed by a trailing newline.

On pluralizing, this is intentionally very literal. The helper appends 's' only when the items array has length strictly greater than 1. So length 0 returns the word unchanged, length 1 also returns the word unchanged, and only arrays with 2 or more elements get the suffix. The suffix is exactly 's', with no 'es' handling or irregular forms.

For index_versions, the key should be the normalized version string, but the value should stay the original record untouched. So if the input record has version 'v1.0.0', the key becomes '1.0.0' but the stored value is still the original {"version":"v1.0.0"} object. Keys should be inserted in the same order as the input array. This reuses the exact normalizeVersion behavior from golden/src/utils/normalize-version.ts to produce the normalized keys. Output here should be compact JSON with no extra whitespace, followed by a trailing newline.

For sorting release keys, valid semver values are ordered highest-to-lowest using semver comparison, but anything that is not valid semver is treated as LOWER than any valid version and placed FIRST before all valid versions. If there are multiple invalid keys, those should be sorted alphabetically ascending with localeCompare. The example to keep in mind is ['garbage', 'moreGarbage', '3.0.0', '1.0.0'] — 'garbage' and 'moreGarbage' come first in alphabetical order, then the valid versions newest-first. Output is one key per line, each terminated by a newline.

Also, one detail on the layout tree builder: the topmost node always uses the 'row' direction by default, and every nested node under that always uses the 'column' direction regardless of the top-level direction. There can be an optional 'direction' parameter in the input, but that can override only the topmost node's direction. The split rule is firstHalf = first Math.floor(n/2) panes (rounding down), secondHalf = remaining panes. And if there is only a single pane, the result should just be that pane identifier as a bare JSON string, not a node object.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: export function positionForRect(target: ClientRect, size: { width: number, height: number }, margin: number = 10): PositionResult

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The fallback placement (when all three candidates fail) uses: left = target.left + target.width/2 - size.width/2 (centered), top = target.top + size.height - margin (NOT target.top + target.height + margin), type = 'bottom'. This is distinct from Candidate 3 which uses top = target.top + target.height + margin.

On the macOS platform (process.platform === 'darwin') only, the top position of the right and left candidates is reduced by margin * 1.5. Specifically, darwinTop = margin * 1.5 (not margin, not 2*margin), and topPlusMargin = target.top - darwinTop. This adjustment only applies to the right and left candidates, NOT to the bottom candidate or the fallback. With a default margin of 10, the adjustment is 15 pixels.

On macOS (process.platform === 'darwin'), when there is more than one button, the first button (index 0, treated as the cancel/dismiss button) is moved from the front to the END of the array while the remaining buttons keep their relative order. On all other platforms, and when there is only one button or zero buttons, the array is returned unchanged. The first element of the input is always treated as the cancel button regardless of its content.

The invertPosition function maps: 'left' -> 'right', 'right' -> 'left', 'top' -> 'bottom', 'bottom' -> 'top'. Any unrecognized or unknown value (including null, undefined, empty string, or any other string like 'xyz') falls back to returning 'top'. The return value is a plain string; output should be followed by a trailing newline.

A candidate placement passes the bounds check when ALL of: (1) left >= 0, (2) top >= 0, (3) left + size.width <= viewport.innerWidth + size.width * 0.3 (horizontal tolerance is 30% of the floating element's width), (4) top + size.height <= viewport.innerHeight + size.height * 0.3 (vertical tolerance is 30% of the floating element's height). Tolerances are based on size dimensions, not viewport dimensions.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the button ordering behavior should mirror the platform-aware sorting we use elsewhere in our dialog management code
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Refers to golden/src/utils/sort-buttons.ts — the sortButtons function which checks process.platform === 'darwin' and moves the first (cancel/dismiss) button to the end on macOS. The 'dialog management code' hint points an agent to grep for sortButtons or dialog-related platform checks.

oracle_data.fallback_response: The question does not match any known constraint for this task. Please rephrase or ask about a specific feature such as version normalization, pluralization, version indexing, version sorting, path navigation, layout building, pane flattening, position inversion, floating element placement, or button ordering.

feature1_normalize_version.json.description: Strip an optional single leading lowercase version-prefix letter from a release identifier. If the identifier begins with that prefix letter, the function returns the identifier with that first character removed; otherwise it returns the identifier unchanged. The output is the resulting string followed by a trailing newline.

feature2_pluralize.json.description: Given a base word and a collection of items, return the word made plural by appending the plural suffix only when the collection holds more than one item; for an empty collection or a single item the base word is returned unchanged. The output is the resulting word followed by a trailing newline.

feature3_index_versions.json.description: Convert a list of release records into a lookup object keyed by each record's normalized version string (the version with any single leading prefix letter removed). Each entry maps the normalized key to the original, unmodified record. Keys appear in the order the records were supplied. The output is the resulting object serialized as compact JSON followed by a trailing newline.

feature4_sort_versions.json.description: Sort the keys of a record of release entries and return them in order. Keys that are valid semantic versions are ordered from highest to lowest. Keys that are not valid semantic versions are considered lower than any valid version and are placed first, ordered among themselves alphabetically. The output is the sorted keys, one per line, each terminated by a newline.

feature5_1_get_at_path.json.description: Read a nested value out of an object using a dot-delimited path. The path is split on dots and each segment is followed in turn, descending one level per segment, and the value found at the end of the path is returned. The output is that value serialized as compact JSON followed by a trailing newline.

feature5_2_set_at_path.json.description: Write a value into a nested object at a location named by a dot-delimited path. The path is split on dots; all but the final segment are followed to descend into the object, and the supplied value is assigned at the final segment, mutating the existing object in place. The output is the whole mutated object serialized as compact JSON followed by a trailing newline.

feature6_1_create_layout.json.description: Build a binary split-pane layout tree from an ordered list of pane identifiers. A single pane yields just that pane identifier as a bare leaf. For more than one pane, the list is divided into a first group containing the first half of the panes (rounding down) and a second group containing the remaining panes, and a node is produced with a split direction and two child slots, each child built recursively from its group. The topmost node uses the row direction; every nested node uses the column direction. The output is the resulting tree serialized as compact JSON followed by a trailing newline.

feature6_2_visible_panes.json.description: Flatten a split-pane layout tree into the ordered list of visible pane identifiers it contains. A null tree yields an empty list. A bare leaf (a single pane identifier) yields a one-element list. A split node is traversed by visiting its first child before its second child, descending recursively, so the panes come out in left-to-right order. The output is the resulting list serialized as a compact JSON array followed by a trailing newline.

feature7_1_invert_position.json.description: Invert a directional placement. The four directions form two opposite pairs: left and right are opposites, and top and bottom are opposites. Given one of these four directions, the function returns its opposite. Any unrecognized value falls back to the top direction. The output is the resulting direction name followed by a trailing newline.

feature7_2_position_for_rect.json.description: Compute a placement for a floating element (such as a tooltip or popover) relative to a target rectangle, keeping it within the visible viewport. Inputs are the target rectangle (its left, top, width, height), the floating element's size (width, height), an optional margin (default 10), the current viewport (its inner width and height), and the platform identifier. The function tries candidate placements in order and returns the first that fits, otherwise a fallback. Candidate 1 (to the right): left = target.left + target.width + margin, top = target.top, type 'right'. Candidate 2 (to the left): left = target.left - margin - size.width, top = target.top, type 'left'. Candidate 3 (centered below): left = target.left + target.width/2 - size.width/2, top = target.top + target.height + margin, type 'bottom'. Fallback: same centered left, top = target.top + size.height - margin, type 'bottom'. A candidate fits when left >= 0, top >= 0, left + size.width does not exceed the viewport inner width plus a horizontal tolerance of 30% of size.width, and top + size.height does not exceed the viewport inner height plus a vertical tolerance of 30% of size.height. On the macOS platform only, the top of the right and left candidates is additionally raised by one and a half margins. The output is the chosen placement, an object with left, top and type, serialized as compact JSON followed by a trailing newline.

feature8_sort_buttons.json.description: Order a list of dialog buttons according to the host platform's convention, where the first button in the input is treated as the cancel/dismiss button. On the macOS platform, when there is more than one button, the dismiss button is moved from the front to the end while the remaining buttons keep their relative order; on every other platform, and whenever there is at most one button, the list is returned unchanged. The output is the resulting list of buttons serialized as compact JSON followed by a trailing newline.
